cataloging_subject.label: The Brut, or The Chronicles of England (Brie/APG1531 selected compilation)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The selected medieval contents printed by Friedrich W. D. Brie in EETS Original Series 131 and 136: the earlier anonymous English prose translation of the French Brut through 1333, followed by a deliberately selected set of independently transmitted English continuations, extracts, appendices, and poems whose selected section K endpoint is 1475. This is not one homogeneous medieval composition and not the entire contents of any one manuscript. Later integration should preserve the edition/selection boundary unless component works are separately researched and identified.

catalog_summary.date: Fourteenth and fifteenth centuries (composite selection; selected narrative endpoint 1475)
genres: Verse chronicle and occasional political verse (components)

date_statements.display: Multiple medieval compositions and continuations, chiefly fourteenth and fifteenth century
notes: No single composition date is justified for the selected compilation.

date_statements.display: Legendary Albina/Brutus through 1475 in the selected content
notes: This is narrative coverage, not a composition-date range. The U-M header's 1479 statement describes a broader continuation tradition and is not projected as this selection's endpoint.

places.label: England
notes: Broad English textual tradition; not a single proved place of composition.
Repository holdings and London publication do not establish a common composition place.

language_statements.label: Latin

scope.description: Scoped entries, phrases, and tabular material; exact component allocation awaits review and is not a whole-selection claim

notes: Modern English headings and apparatus are editorial and intentionally excluded from work-level language facets.

form_statements.description: The common Brut and most continuations are prose, but the edition contains 64 line groups and 897 encoded verse lines; poetry is substantive, not merely incidental typography.

genre_statements.label: Verse chronicle and occasional political verse (components)

summaries.value: An edition-level selected compilation of the Middle English prose Brut through 1333 and independently transmitted continuations, extracts, appendices, and poems selected by Friedrich W. D. Brie. The selected text ends in 1475 and includes substantive prose and verse, including John Page's Siege of Rouen poem within section D; U-M retains the selection as APG1531 while omitting most carrier paratext.

Fifteen ordered edition/XML components occupy printed pages 1-289 and 291-604. Printed page 290 is absent; the encoding follows carrier PDF pages 23-311 and 331-644 and excludes PDF pages 312-330 of Part II paratext.

The record describes Brie's selected edition-level compilation, not one homogeneous anonymous medieval work and not the contents of one manuscript. The selected section K closes in 1475; the U-M header's 1479 wording is retained only as a qualified conflict about the broader continuation tradition.

extent.description: Fifteen mutually exclusive edition/XML components (DIV2) are counted once. Their form accounting totals 8 prose-only, 2 verse-only, and 5 mixed components. XML representation metrics total 64 LG and 897 L; the nested John Page analysis overlaps component D and is not counted again.

content_parts.label: John Page's Siege of Rouen
content_parts.summary: John Page's verse account of the Siege of Rouen, nested within section D only.
notes: Overlaps count-bearing component D, so item_count is null and its verse metrics are not added a second time.
John Page's authorship does not extend to the prose framing or the rest of section D.

related_works.title: French prose Brut tradition
notes: Component A is an anonymous English translation of this tradition; the relation does not extend that translation claim to the independently transmitted later components, and no physical exemplar is identified.

related_works.title: The Chronicles of England (William Caxton, 1480)
notes: Brie collated section G against this printing; it is not the source of the whole selected compilation.

open_questions.question: Which selected continuations, extracts, and poems should be identified as independent works rather than edition components?
next_steps: Research each component against current Brut scholarship before splitting; keep this packet's edition-level boundary meanwhile.
notes: Blocking for: component-level work manifests

open_questions.question: Can any anonymous continuation be assigned more specifically and with current scholarly support?
next_steps: Consult current Brut manuscript scholarship; do not generalize John Page's authorship beyond the Siege of Rouen poem.
notes: Blocking for: fine-grained authorship

next_steps: Inspect the current physical/digital reprint before adding a reprints or version relation; for now it is only an access option.
notes: Blocking for: reprint lineage relation

This is not one homogeneous medieval composition and not the entire contents of any one manuscript. Later integration should preserve the edition/selection boundary unless component works are separately researched and identified.
The selected content ends in 1475. The header's 1479 statement is retained only as an attributed conflict concerning a broader Brut-continuation summary.
Printed page 290 is absent from both the edition pagination and the encoding; it is not an accidentally omitted encoded page.
Modern English editorial headings and apparatus do not make the medieval selection an English/Modern-English bilingual work; scoped Latin remains an explicit research question.
The repository artifact, U-M encoding, Brie edition, physical witnesses, and provider reproductions remain separate lineage and rights layers.
